title: Spinning Border Leicester wool
subtitle: A lustrous, long-stapled British breed that spins a smooth, weighty yarn for wovens and outerwear
excerpt: Border Leicester produces a long-stapled, lustrous fleece with a pronounced crimp and a natural sheen that persists in the finished yarn. It spins best as a worsted-style yarn and is especially suited to weaving warps and outerwear knits where durability and lustre are wanted.
sourceNotes: Border Leicester breed characteristics documented in British wool-breed literature and spinning guild references. The breed was developed in the 18th century from Leicester Longwool stock by the Culley brothers in Northumberland.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool with a natural sheen caused by a smooth, flat surface on each fibre scale. Border Leicester is a British lustre longwool; the sheen persists in the spun yarn and woven cloth.
The length of an individual lock of fleece. Border Leicester staple runs 12 to 20 cm, making it one of the longest-stapled British breeds and easy to draft on a wheel.
A spinning technique with a short, controlled drafting zone that keeps fibres parallel. The smooth fibre surface of Border Leicester suits a worsted approach.

content.text: and a strong natural shine in the spun yarn. It suits a
well because the long fibres stay parallel when combed. A woollen approach gives a loftier yarn but the sheen does not survive the carding.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Border Leicester tops or raw fleece locks

content.text: Flick each lock open at the tips with a flicker brush to loosen any matted fibres at the cut end. Border Leicester locks are long and can tangle at the tip if the fleece was handled wet. Do not over-flick; two or three strokes per tip is enough. If working from commercially prepared tops, split each length of tops in half lengthways before drafting to reduce the bulk per drafting zone.

Set the wheel to a lower twist ratio than you would use for Merino (8:1 to 10:1). Border Leicester fibres are long and smooth; they need less twist per centimetre than short, crimpy wools. Spin Z-twist with a worsted draw, keeping the drafting zone 5 to 8 cm long. The singles will have a slight natural sheen even before plying.

Ply two singles with S-twist to produce a balanced 2-ply. The long fibres in Border Leicester mean an undertwisted ply will pull apart easily in use; err toward a slightly firmer ply than you might use for Merino. Set the twist by soaking in warm water and snapping between your hands, then hang with a substantial weight to encourage the sheen-enhancing parallel fibre alignment.

items.problem: The sheen has disappeared and the yarn looks dull
items.cause: Over-twisting the singles packs the fibres so tightly that the smooth fibre surface cannot reflect light
items.fix: Reduce the twist ratio and spin at a slower, more even cadence. Border Leicester needs less twist than most other breeds; the sheen is a sign of correct twist level.

items.problem: The very long locks kept wrapping around the orifice hook
items.cause: The lock was fed in at the wrong angle or in too large a bundle
items.fix: Hold the lock parallel to the orifice and feed it in from the side rather than straight on. Work smaller bundles and keep the drafting zone short.
